passages.explanation: Nicodemus is a Pharisee and a member of the Jewish ruling council — a powerful, educated, religious insider. He comes to Jesus at night, which suggests he doesn't want to be seen. He opens respectfully, acknowledging that Jesus must be from God because no one could do what he does without God's backing. He's cautious but genuinely curious.

Jesus cuts straight past the pleasantries and tells Nicodemus that no one can even see God's Kingdom unless they are 'born anew' — meaning a complete spiritual rebirth, not a physical one. Nicodemus takes it literally and asks how an old man can re-enter his mother's womb. Jesus explains: physical birth gives you physical life, but you also need a spiritual birth from the Holy Spirit to enter God's Kingdom. He compares the Spirit to wind — you can hear it and see its effects, but you can't control where it comes from or where it goes. Spiritual rebirth works the same way: it's real but not something you can manufacture or predict.

Nicodemus still doesn't understand, and Jesus is blunt: you're supposed to be Israel's teacher and you can't grasp this? Jesus compares himself to the bronze serpent Moses lifted up in the wilderness — anyone who looked at it was healed, and in the same way, anyone who believes in Jesus will have eternal life. Verse 16 states the core claim directly: God loved the world so much that he gave his Son so that believers wouldn't perish. Jesus says he came to save, not to judge — but people who reject him are effectively judging themselves because they're choosing darkness over the light that has been offered to them. People who do evil avoid the light because it exposes them; people living honestly welcome it.

Jesus and his disciples move into the Judean countryside and begin baptising. Meanwhile John the Baptist is still baptising nearby. John's disciples get concerned — they tell him that the man he testified about is now baptising too, and everyone is going to Jesus instead of them. They're feeling threatened by Jesus' growing popularity.

John the Baptist shuts down the jealousy immediately. He reminds his disciples that he told them plainly he is not the Messiah — he was sent ahead to prepare the way. He compares himself to the best man at a wedding: the best man isn't the groom, and he doesn't compete with the groom. He's there to support him and he's happy doing it. Then John states plainly: 'He must increase, but I must decrease.' He's accepting that his role is to step back as Jesus' ministry grows.

This passage — likely John the author's commentary rather than the Baptist still speaking — separates Jesus from everyone else. Jesus comes from heaven and is above all. He speaks what he has directly seen and heard from God, and God has given him the Spirit without any limit. The Father has placed everything under the Son's authority. The chapter ends with a stark choice: believe in the Son and have eternal life, or reject him and remain under God's judgment. There is no neutral middle ground.
